title: Intel Just Ripped 11% — CPUs Are Suddenly the Most Dangerous Trade on Wall Street
subtitle: Google and Nvidia are knocking on Intel's foundry door. The stock is up 450% in 12 months. The bears just got absolutely smoked. And the CEO says CPUs are now the backbone of AI.
summary: Intel surged 11.2% Monday as the CPU renaissance narrative took hold. Google and Nvidia reportedly tapping Intel Foundry. Bears crushed in massive short squeeze. From $19 to $110 in a year — this is the turnaround of 2026.

image.caption: Intel's manufacturing facilities represent the renewed bet on CPU-driven AI inference at scale.

links.label: Intel Stock Quote on Yahoo Finance →

bodyHtml:
Intel just ripped +11.2%. The top stock in the S&P 500 on Monday. Closed at $110.27. If you're still running the 'Intel missed mobile, Intel missed AI' script from 2024, you've already lost the trade.

This thing was $19 a year ago. Let that sink in. YTD +150%. Twelve-month return: +450%. That's not a bounce. That's a full-blown resurrection.

The Narrative Just Inverted

For two years the Street's thesis was simple: GPUs are the only game in AI. Nvidia owns everything. Intel's a dinosaur. CPUs are legacy silicon headed for the museum.

That thesis got absolutely torched on Monday.

Intel's CEO dropped the line that's now echoing across every trading desk: "CPUs are the backbone of AI." Inference workloads — the stuff that actually runs AI at scale — runs better on CPUs than GPUs. Agentic AI, the next wave everyone's scrambling for, is CPU-native. The Xeon clusters that Wall Street called obsolete six months ago? They're suddenly the most valuable real estate in semis.

Inference at scale doesn't need H100s. It needs Xeon clusters. And Intel owns that lane.

Foundry Is the Real Story

Here's where it gets wild. Google and Nvidia are reportedly turning to Intel Foundry for AI chip manufacturing. Let that sit for a second — Nvidia, the company that ate Intel's lunch for two years, might be paying Intel to make its chips.

The Motley Fool reported the Google AI chip foundry order. Intel's 18A process is suddenly the hottest ticket in semiconductor manufacturing. TSMC's got a legit competitor, and the hyperscalers want second-source capacity yesterday.

This isn't speculative hype. Citi and Evercore both hiked price targets. These aren't meme-chasing analysts — they're the same desks that buried Intel two years ago.

The Short Squeeze of 2026

The bears just got steamrolled. Intel ripped as high as 14% intraday before settling at +11.2%. That's not organic buying — that's a brutal short squeeze lighting up every margin call on the Street. Headlines were calling it outright: "Intel Rips 14% Higher: Apple Foundry Reports Crush Bears in Massive Short Squeeze."

And the supply-demand dynamics are getting ridiculous. 24/7 Wall St ran the headline: "CPU Shortage Is So Bad, Even Intel's 'Scrap' Is Turning Into Gold." When the scraps are printing, you know the cycle has flipped.

Broadcom Who?

Here's the competitive reality nobody's talking about. While Broadcom just vaporized $300 billion in market cap, Intel is eating their lunch. The AI narrative is rotating from GPU scarcity to CPU abundance. Inference economics favor cheap, dense, power-efficient CPU clusters over $40,000 GPU racks. That math doesn't reverse.

Can This Keep Going?

The honest answer: it depends on 18A execution. If the foundry ramp stumbles, this whole trade unwinds fast. But here's the thing — if Google and Nvidia are actually committing foundry volume, Intel's revenue trajectory just bent upward permanently. This isn't a cyclical bounce in PC demand. This is structural.

Intel went from 'left for dead' to the most dangerous momentum trade on the board. $110 is no longer a meme. The question isn't whether Intel's back — it's whether $150 is the floor or the ceiling.

— The Signal
